495

sections.title: Active Ingredient Management
sections.text:
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Transaction MB5M, which is the shelf life list, can also be used to gain comprehensive
visibility into materials whose shelf lives have ended or are about to end (see Figure
10.37).
Figure 10.37 Shelf Life Expiration List
The next section is on the configuration, master data, and business process of active
ingredient management in SAP S/4HANA.
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Active ingredient management in batch management enables organizations to take
advantage of material valuation at the batch level, as well as the potency or active propor-
tion in the overall quantity of material. This functionality is especially helpful when
you’re using an active element or ingredient of a material that contains carrier materials,
including impurities. If the total physical quantity of a material is a mixture, then the
active or potent material within the mixture is known as the active ingredient quantity.
This section begins by covering configuration basics and important master data that
forms an integral part in running the end-to-end business processes that involve active
ingredient management. Finally, for business processes in active ingredient manage-
ment, we run an entire procurement process that consists of creating a purchase order
for an active ingredient material, moving to goods receipt, recording results, making
usage decisions, and posting stock.

10 Batch Management
496

sections.title: Configuration Basics
sections.text:
10.7.1 Configuration Basics
In this section, we’ll list the most important settings and steps necessary to implement
batch ingredient management in procurement.
Activating Active Ingredient Management
To activate ingredient management functionality at the client level, you use Transac-
tion OMWS or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management 
Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure  Activate Batch-Specific Material Units of
Measure. You choose the Active radio button to ensure that batch management is
active and then save your settings.
Creating a Unit of Measure for Proportion Units
For batch-specific units of measure, you need to add a characteristic to the batch class
that carries the conversion factor for deriving the alternative batch-specific unit of mea-
sure. You’ll likely need to create this unit of measure before proceeding further, but if
the unit of measure already exists in the system, then you can continue to the next step.
To create a unit of measure for an active ingredient that should act as a proportion unit
of measure, you use Transaction CUNI or follow menu path SAP IMG  SAP NetWeaver 
General Settings  Check Units of Measurement. Choose the Units of Measurement (no
dimensions) button and then choose Create. For this example, on the screen shown in
Figure 10.38, we create a unit of measure called AIU and enter minimal details, such as
its commercial and technical names, its description, and its decimal places, if any.
Figure 10.38 Details of Unit of Measure for Ratio Unit
© 

497
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Proportion Unit
The next step is to maintain an entry of a batch-specific unit of measure and create a
relationship with an available standard unit of measure. To do so, you use Transaction
BMA1 or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch-
Specific Material Units of Measure  Edit Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure. Cre-
ate a new entry for the unit of measure called AIU, which the system should be able to
use as a batch-specific unit of measure. Enter “KG” for the unit of measure (UoM) as the
reference unit of measure (see Figure 10.39).
Figure 10.39 Batch-Specific Unit of Measure
Proportional Factors
You can maintain a relationship between the characteristic’s unit of measure, batch-
specific unit of measure, and base unit of measure. To do so, you use Transaction BMA3
or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch-Specific
Material Units of Measure  Calculate Proportional Factors  Define Calculation of Propor-
tion Quantity from Base Quantity. Choose New Entries and enter the Commercial format,
Batch-specific UoM, and Base Unit of Measure information, as shown in Figure 10.40.
Figure 10.40 Calculation of Proportion/Production Quantity from Base Quantity

10 Batch Management
498
Tips and Tricks
You can also have a separate Batch-specific UoM in Figure 10.40 that may differ from
both the base unit of measure and the characteristic unit of measure. The steps to cre-
ate a batch-specific unit of measure are the same as those we’ve already covered. Once
you’ve created this unit of measure, you can assign the batch-specific unit of measure
as shown in Figure 10.40.

sections.title: Master Data
sections.text:
10.7.2 Master Data
Let’s now cover the master data setup that you need to perform to ensure that active
ingredient management integrates with your business processes.
Defining a Ratio Unit of Measure/Conversion Factor Characteristic
We’ll now maintain the conversion factor characteristic for the batch class. Use Trans-
action CT04 to create a characteristic and then assign the same characteristic to the
batch class of the product that requires the batch-specific conversion. For this example,
you create characteristic P03 with the description “Fat Content” and with the unit of
measure set as “%.” The characteristic that you create should be in numeric format and
can’t have multiple values or an interval. All the data you need to define for this char-
acteristic is defined in Figure 10.41.
Figure 10.41 Class Characteristic
© 

499
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Choose the Values tab in Figure 10.41 to bring up the screen shown in Figure 10.42,
where you enter a characteristic value range of “0.00–100.00” and check the box to set
the default (D) indicator. In the Addnl data (additional data) tab, choose Display Values.
Finally, in the Restrictions tab, enter classes “022” and “023” so that this class character-
istic (P03) is available for use only in these two classes.
Figure 10.42 Values Tab in Characteristic
Assigning a Characteristic to the Class
Use Transaction CL02 and assign the characteristic (conversion factor characteristic
P03) as shown in Figure 10.43.
Figure 10.43 Class for Active Ingredient Management with Organizational Area

10 Batch Management
500
In addition to assigning the characteristic to the class, you need to classify the batch
class in the header as a special batch class by entering a value of “S” (substance steel) in
the class header of the Organizational area field.
Next, choose the Char. (characteristic) tab. Here, you also need to add the value “S” in
the Org. Areas column for characteristic P03 in the characteristics screen of the class
(see Figure 10.44). This step ensures that the system uses this characteristic in carrying
out the batch-specific units of measure functionality. Additionally, be sure to also
check the Prop./Prod. Unit Rel (proportional/production unit relevant) box to denote
that characteristic P03 will be used for the purpose of calculating the active ingredient
or potency of a material.
Figure 10.44 Characteristics Tab in Class
Inspection Setup: Class Characteristic
Next, create a master inspection characteristic and link it to class characteristic P03.
Use Transaction QS21 or follow menu path Logistics  Quality Management  Quality
Planning  Basic Data  Inspection Characteristic  Create.
On the initial screen that appears, enter master inspection characteristic “AIUMIC13”
and plant “1710,” and in the Class Charc field, enter “P03,” which is the class characteris-
tic you created in the previous step. Figure 10.45 shows that the system automatically
copies all the information from Class Charc P03 to Master IC AIUMIC13, including con-
trol indicators and upper and lower values.
Next, choose Control Indicators and then Quant. Data to verify the completeness and
correctness of the information.
Figure 10.46 shows the quantitative data indicating that the system automatically picks
up values from class characteristic P03, not just for the Decimal Places (2 places) and its
unit of measure (Msmt Unit %) but also for the upper and lower limits that we main-
tained previously (shown in Figure 10.41).
© 

501
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Figure 10.45 Class Characteristic in Master Inspection Characteristic
Figure 10.46 Define Master Inspection Characteristics
Maintaining Active Ingredient Data for a Material
Next, assign batch class P03 to the material master, which requires the functionality of
batch-specific units of measure. For this example, assign class 023 and then assign
batch class P03 to material 2242.

10 Batch Management
502
To maintain data for a material, you update the classification view of material master
2242 (fatty acid concentrate that is managed in batches) with Transaction MM02 or
menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Environment  Material
Master  Material  Change  Immediately. Go to the classification view and assign batch
class P03 to material 2242, as shown in Figure 10.47. You don’t need to assign any char-
acteristic value range in the Value field: the system automatically picks up this infor-
mation because earlier, you set the Default value settings (shown in Figure 10.41).
Figure 10.47 Active Ingredient Class Defined in Material Master’s Classification View
Maintaining Unit of Measure Data in the Material Master
You maintain the Additional Data view to maintain the conversion factor between the
active ingredient unit of measure (AIU) and the base unit of measure, but also with pro-
portion or product units of measure. Use Transaction MM02, enter material 2242,
choose the Basic data 1 view, and navigate to Additional Data.
Figure 10.48 shows the Units of measure tab of material 2242. Here, you maintain a con-
version factor between base unit of measure (kg) and AIU. For this example, we main-
tain that for every 100 kg of material, we have 85 AIU; that is, 100 kg = 85 AIU (or 85 AIU
for every 100 kg).
Next, choose the Proportion/prod.unit tab on the far right of the screen, and the screen
in Figure 10.49 will appear. Here, first select A for Units meas. use from the dropdown list
that denotes the proportion units. Then, enter previously created class characteristic
© 

503
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
P03 and assign a plan value of 85%, which is within the allowed range for the class char-
acteristic. This will indicate to the system that every 100 kg of fatty acid concentrate
material should contain 85 units of the active ingredient. Then, for the proportion/prod-
uct unit, enter the AIU value, which is the new proportion unit in which the system mea-
sures or calculates the active ingredient. Also, check the V-R box, which is for valuation
at the unit of measure, so that the system knows that all valuations will be in this unit of
measure (AIU). These are still all planned values; we’ll show you later how the inspection
results in QM enable the system to make adjustment entries to account for the actual
content of the active ingredient in the material. Lastly, choose Main Data and then the
purchasing view.
Figure 10.48 Units of Measure Conversion
Figure 10.49 Proportion/Product Unit of Measure Tab in Additional Data of Material Master

10 Batch Management
504
Tips and Tricks
If the Proportion/prod.unit tab shown in Figure 10.49 doesn’t appear in your SAP
S/4HANA system, then you need to first maintain the step shown in the Purchasing
view in the material master (see the next subsection) and then come back to Figure
10.49, wherein the Proportion/prod.unit tab will appear. This is because the Proportion/
prod.unit tab will only appear when the Plant organizational level is selected for a
material master at the time of material master creation or change.
Purchasing View in Material Master
In the purchasing view (see Figure 10.50), check the boxes for both of the Batch
Management indicators. Then, enter “AIU” for the Order Unit so that you can place the
purchase order with the supplier in the active ingredient unit of measure. Also, enter
“1” for Var. OUn to enable the system to allow the creation of purchase orders in the
alternative unit of measure.
Figure 10.50 Purchasing View of Material Master
Valuation Category of Material
Figure 10.51 shows the Accounting 1 tab. Here, you enter “X” in the Valuation Category
field to ensure that the system valuates the material at the batch level.
© 

505
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Figure 10.51 Valuation Category
Next, set the Price control indicator to “V” to denote a moving average price. With a
moving average price, the system updates the material’s price with every goods receipt
and with invoice verification.
Tips and Tricks
If you need to activate valuation category X as shown in Figure 10.51, follow menu path
SAP IMG  Materials Management  Valuation & Account Assignment  Split Valuation 
Configure Split Valuation and follow these steps:
1. Choose Execute and then choose Cancel.
2. Choose Global Categories. You’ll see valuation category X Automat (Batch); choose
X and then choose the Change button. On this screen, check the Determine Val.
type automat. box. By default, this box is checked—but if it isn’t, check it and save.
3. Click Back twice.
4. Choose Local Definitions. You’ll see your plant, so choose the plant number (in this
example, this is plant 1710) and click the Cats  OU button.
5. Choose X and then click the Activate button. The system will activate valuation cat-
egory X for the requisite plant, and you’ll see Active in the Status column beside
Valuation Category X.
6. Save your settings.

10 Batch Management
506
QM Inspection Setup: Inspection Type
In the Quality Management tab of the material master, you need to maintain the set-
tings shown in Figure 10.52 to trigger automatic inspection lot creation based on a
goods receipt for a purchase order of this raw material. The activated InspType (inspec-
tion type) should be of type 01. Check the Insp. with Mat. Spec. box to denote that the
system will look for a material specification at the time of inspection lot creation for
goods receipt for the purchase order. Alternatively, you can also use an inspection plan
(by checking the Insp. with Task List box) for this material for sample calculation,
results recording, and usage decisions. Save your entries.
Figure 10.52 Inspection Setup
QM Inspection Setup: Material Specification
To create a material specification, use Transaction QS61 or follow menu path Logistics 
Quality Management  Quality Planning  Inspection Planning  Material Specification 
Edit. On the initial screen, enter material 2242 and press (Enter) to view the screen
shown in Figure 10.53.
The system automatically copies all information from class characteristic P03 and mas-
ter inspection characteristic AIUMIC13, including upper and lower limits. Because all
information in the material specification refers to either the class characteristic or the
master inspection characteristic (look for the lock Ref icon in Figure 10.53), you can
make hardly any changes to the material specification.
© 

507
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Figure 10.53 Material Specification of Material 2242 with Master Inspection Characteristic
AIUMIC13
Standard Price for Batch
Apart from the material’s standard or moving average price, you can also maintain a
standard price for the batch-specific unit of measure that the system keeps updating
with each transaction, such as goods receipt or invoice verification. To maintain a stan-
dard price for the batch-specific unit of measure, use Transaction MWB1 or follow
menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch-Specific Units of
Measure  Standard Price  Create. Enter the standard price and price unit.
In the initial screen that appears, enter Material “2242,” Plant “1710,” and proportion/prod-
uct (Prop./Prod.) “AIU.” Then, press (Enter), and the screen in Figure 10.54 will appear.
Figure 10.54 Proportion Valuation

10 Batch Management
508
Here, the system refers to the data from the Accounting 1 view of the material master.
In the Current Proportion Valuation area, you can maintain the standard price and
price unit.

sections.title: Business Processes
sections.text:
10.7.3 Business Processes
Let’s now proceed to the actual business processes of active ingredient management.
We first create a purchase order of the same material (2242) and record its goods
receipt, and then, we perform results recording, which consists of recording the details
of the actual ingredient proportion, making a usage decision, and posting the mate-
rial’s stock. We also show how the SAP S/4HANA system keeps updating the informa-
tion in each step of the business process.
Purchase Order
Before you create a purchase order for material 2242, quickly refer to Figure 10.50,
which shows the purchasing view of the material where you set the (purchase) Order
Unit to be the AIU. If you create the purchase orders in active ingredient quantities, the
system updates the purchase order quantity and physical quantity in the purchase
order history. In this way, you can ensure greater control of and visibility into the
degree of order quantity fulfillment based on active ingredient quantities.
To create a purchase order for material 2242 that contains an active ingredient mea-
sured in the AIU proportion, use Transaction ME21N or follow menu path Logistics 
Materials Management  Purchasing  Purchase Order  Create  Supplier/Supplying
Plant Known. Enter Supplier “EWM17-SU01,” Material “2242,” a PO Quantity of “100
AIU,” a Net Price of “100 USD,” and a Plant of “Plant1 US.” (You can also enter plant code
1710, in which case, the system will use the plant’s description instead of its code.) In the
Quantities/Weights tab, the system automatically converts not just the purchase order
quantity into a stock-keeping unit (which is kg, and in which you maintain the mate-
rial’s inventory) but also shows the conversion factor between order units and stock
keeping units. This is the same information that you maintained previously in Figure
10.50.
Choose the Delivery tab and the screen in Figure 10.55 appears. It accounts for the fact
that the quantity of an active ingredient that the supplier actually delivers may vary
slightly from the quantity ordered. Therefore, you need to maintain the tolerances for
under- and overdelivery. For this example, check the Unlimited box to denote unlim-
ited overdelivery; for underdelivery, enter 20%. Then, you save the purchase order, and
in this example, the system creates a purchase order with the number 4500001882.
© 

509
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Figure 10.55 Delivery Tolerance in Purchase Order
Tips and Tricks
You can maintain the under- and overdelivery tolerance for a material by assigning the
purchasing value key in the purchasing view of the material master (see Figure 10.50).
Goods Receipts for Purchase Orders
You can post the goods receipt with reference to the purchase order in the base unit of
measure. To do so, use Transaction MIGO or follow menu path Logistics  Materials
Management  Inventory Management  Goods Movement  Goods Receipt. Choose
Goods Receipt (A01) and then Purchase Order (R01) and then enter the purchase order
number you created in the previous step. The system will then bring up all the requisite
information directly from the purchase order (see Figure 10.56).
The base unit of measure for material 2242 is kg. You can determine the physical quan-
tity of the material only at the time of goods receipt. In this example, the supplier deliv-
ers 117.647 kg. If the active ingredient proportion of the batch is not known at the time
of goods receipt, then you record the goods receipt per the planned proportion; that is,
you assume that the supplier’s delivered material of 117.647 kg does indeed contain 100
AIU. In active ingredient management, the actual proportion is taken from the usage
decision, and the system prompts you to make a subsequent debit or credit entry
(adjustment entry) for the purchase order, which we’ll show later. Now, save your entry,
and the system will create a material document to record the goods receipt for this pur-
chase order.

10 Batch Management
510
Figure 10.56 Goods Receipt of a Purchase Order
Displaying Purchase Order History
To display the purchase order history, use Transaction ME23N or follow menu path
Logistics  Materials Management  Purchasing  Purchase Order  Display. On the Item
Detail screen, choose the Purchase Order History tab and the system will display the
goods receipt (see Figure 10.57). The system first assumes that the ordered quantity was
delivered (100 AIU). Figure 10.57 shows that the system updates the history of goods
receipt for the purchase order.
Figure 10.57 Purchase Order History
© 

511
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Displaying the Active Ingredient Valuation Price
Because the active ingredient of material 2242 has its own valuation price, you can
check whether the system updated its valuation record based on the latest or last goods
receipts. Use Transaction MWB3 or follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions 
Batch Management  Batch-Specific Units of Measurement  Standard Price  Display.
The screen shown in Figure 10.58 shows that the system updates the material valua-
tion, especially the moving average price (V), because we have set a “V” indicator for
Price Control.
Figure 10.58 Displaying Active Ingredient Valuation Price
Displaying the Material’s Price
To check whether the system updates the price per the ingredient management based
on the latest goods receipt, you can display the average price, standard price, and total
stock of the active ingredient material in the Accounting 1 tab, in which the valuation
type represents the batch level.
Use Transaction MM03 to display the material’s price. The system displays the price per
base unit of measure, and this price assumes that the base unit of measure contains the
same active ingredient proportion as specified in the planned value. The Val. (valua-
tion) Type of the material is at the batch level and is for batch number 122.

10 Batch Management
512
Quality Inspection: Results Recording
The goods receipt for material 2242 now appears as an inspection lot. By this time, it
makes sense for you to have measured the potency, or the actual quantity of the active
ingredient in the delivered material. You can enter the results of the inspection lot with
Transaction QE01 and enter the inspection lot number (which in this example is
“10000000725”) and batch number “122.”
On the screen in Figure 10.59, enter a value of “65” (for 65%) as the result for this inspec-
tion lot and save it. This results recording denotes that although you expected the sup-
plier to deliver material consisting of 85% active ingredients, the actual percentage of
active ingredients found (after necessary tests) was 65%. Save your entries.
Figure 10.59 Results Recording
Quality Inspection: Usage Decision
To record the usage decision, use Transaction QA11 and enter inspection lot number
10000000725. In the Characteristics tab, enter an acceptance without further action
(UD code A and code group 01). Choose the Inspection Lot Stock, and the screen in
Figure 10.60 will appear. Here, post the goods receipt quantity (117.647 KG) to unre-
stricted stock and save it.
Once you’re done saving, the system brings up an information message that stipulates
that you need to correct the purchase order history for inspection lot 10000000725 by
using Transaction MWBQ. The system brings this message up each time it finds a differ-
ence between the theoretical (planned) and actual values of an active ingredient (see
Figure 10.61).
© 

513
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Figure 10.60 Inspection Stock Posting
Figure 10.61 Information Message to Correct Purchase Order History
Correcting the Purchase Order History
To correct the purchase order history for the inspection lot, use Transaction MWBQ or
follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch-Specific
Units of Measure  Subsequent Adjustment f. Goods Receipt  Enter with Ref. to Insp.
Lot. Enter inspection lot number 10000000725, the document date, and the posting
date, and then choose Execute. Then, the system will bring up a message confirming
successful posting to the account, and it will correct the purchase order history based
on the inspection lot results and the usage decision (see Figure 10.62).

10 Batch Management
514
Figure 10.62 Material Document Creation on Purchase Order History Update
Displaying the Purchase Order History
Let’s now look at the updated purchase order history and check out the physical and
financial adjustment entries.
To display the updated purchase order history, use Transaction ME23N. On the Item
Detail subscreen, choose the Purchase Order History tab (see Figure 10.63). The system
displays not just the original goods receipt with movement type 101 but also subse-
quent adjustments of the purchase order using movement type 142. It also updates the
purchase order quantity to 76.471 AIU by subtracting (deducting) a quantity of 23.529
AIU from the ordered and delivered quantity of 100 AIU. Similarly, the financial value is
reduced by 2,352.90 USD to come up with the revised value of 7,647.10 USD.
Figure 10.63 Displaying Purchase Order History
Let’s now delve into how and why the system made these stock and financial adjust-
ments. As explained earlier, 100 kg of material is expected to have a planned propor-
tion of 85 AIU (85%). In this case, and after results recording, the actual percentage of
© 

515
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
the active ingredient was found and recorded to be 65%. Hence, the system undertakes
the following calculation:
((100 AIU × 0.65) ÷ 0.85) = 76.471 AIU
This is the quantity of the active ingredient that the supplier has delivered and should
accordingly be paid for. Because it costs 100 USD for each unit (AIU) of material, we get
100 USD/KG × 76.471 KG = 7,647.10 USD. Also, the delivered percentage of the active
ingredient for the material is less than expected (65% actual, as compared to 85%
expected), so the system uses movement type 142 to make necessary quantity and
financial adjustments. Had this been the other way around, with the supplier deliver-
ing a greater proportion of the active ingredient than ordered (any active ingredient
percentage greater than 85.00%), the system would have made use of movement type
141 to make positive adjustment entries.
Batch Master Record
During the inspection process, the system valuates the batch based on batch characteris-
tics. To display the batch classification, you use Transaction MSC3N or follow menu path
Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch  Display and then choose the
Classification tab (see Figure 10.64). The batch characteristic data shows the transfer of
inspection results as 65%. The batch has a value that differs from the planned value for
the active ingredient characteristic that we maintained as 85% in the material master.
Figure 10.64 Display Batch Master with Active Ingredient Value

10 Batch Management
516
Tips and Tricks
You can also implement a batch search strategy (or batch determination) in your pro-
duction or sales processes so that the system automatically selects the batches with
the requisite or predefined active ingredient content. We already discussed batch
determination in Section 10.5.
Stock Overview
Figure 10.65 1 shows the stock overview of material 2242 of batch number 122, and you
can access it by using Transaction MMBE. Here, you can display the current stock of
material in the base unit of measure (kg) as well as in the AIU of measure 2.
Figure 10.65 Stock Overview in Base Unit of Measure and Active Ingredient Unit of Measure
© 

517
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Logistics Invoice Verification and Release
You can post and release the supplier invoice for the delivered quantity of material and
for the active ingredient. To post the supplier invoice, use Transaction MIRO or follow
menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Logistics Invoice Verification  Docu-
ment Entry  Enter Invoice. Choose the Basic Data tab and enter data for the purchase
order document number, invoice date, reference, amount, and tax data. The system
will accept the invoice even though the amount specified is higher than planned
because there was an overdelivery for the purchase order; the overdelivery was made
possible due to the tolerance entered for the purchase order item. Then, save your
entry and the system will issue an invoice number as well a warning message to inform
you that the invoice is blocked for payment.
Figure 10.66 shows the purchase order history, which also now includes invoice post-
ing.
Figure 10.66 Invoice Posting
To release the invoice so that you can pay the supplier for the delivered material, use
Transaction MRBR or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Logistics
Invoice Verification  Document Entry  Enter Invoice. Enter the invoice document num-
ber and run the report. The system will show the details report, from which you can
select the relevant line item and choose Release to release the invoice.
Due to subsequent adjustment for goods receipt in the purchase order value for mate-
rial 2242, the system will correspondingly update the moving average price of material
2242 of batch number 122. This is shown in Figure 10.67, wherein the Val. type field is
basically the batch number.

10 Batch Management
518
Figure 10.67 Price Update after Invoice Verification
The next section is on batch derivation, which entails an automatic transfer of the clas-
sification data of one batch to another batch of the same or different materials, thereby
saving you time and effort, as well as minimizing data entry errors.

sections.title: Batch Derivation
sections.text:
10.8 Batch Derivation
With batch derivation, a user can efficiently and automatically transfer a batch’s charac-
teristics values from the sender material’s batches to the receiver material’s batches,
thereby saving time, data entry effort, and cost. Batch derivation enables a user to main-
tain one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-one relationships between the sender’s and
the receiver’s materials for complete visibility of information and data.
Whenever a batch-managed material is processed in the production process, its charac-
teristics change and the batch-managed material attains different physical and chemi-
cal attributes. These changed attributes are maintained as characteristic values of the
© 

519
10.8 Batch Derivation
batch-managed materials. With batch derivation, you can control how the system man-
ages the characteristic values flowing from a sender material to a receiver material.
In this section, we’ll start by covering the basic configuration settings needed to activate
batch derivation. Then, we’ll move on to the master data you need to set up, including
settings in the material master, classification, and batch derivation–specific settings.
Finally, we’ll cover the end-to-end business process, in which we show how the settings
made for master data are reflected in the batch derivation process. For this example, the
sender material is 2265 (a raw material that is consumed during the production process
to produce finished good 2264) and the receiver material is 2264 (a finished good).
Let’s start with the configuration basics of batch derivation.

sections.title: Configuration Basics
sections.text:
10.8.1 Configuration Basics
You activate batch derivation in the configuration settings by following menu path SAP
IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Derivation of Batch Data  Activate
Batch Derivation or by using Transaction DVSP. On the screen that appears, select the
Derivation Active radio button and save the settings.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the end-to-end business process of setting up
batch derivation master data, which entails setting up the details of the sender and
receiver materials of a batch’s data. We’ll then use an example to show how the sender
material’s batch data automatically flows into a receiving material’s batch data
through the batch derivation process.

sections.title: Classification in Material Master
sections.text:
10.8.2 Classification in Material Master
In the material master of the sender material and receiver material, you can maintain
the classification view, which incorporates class type 023, which is specific to batch-
managed materials. To maintain the classification view or make changes to the already
maintained classification view in the material master, follow menu path Logistics 
Materials Management  Material Master  Change  Immediately or use Transaction
MM02 (see Figure 10.68).
In Figure 10.68, you can see ZPP of Class Type 023 (Batch) and three characteristics
whose descriptions are given as Thickness, Product Class, and Date of Manufacture. On
this screen, you simply assign an already-created class, which in this example is ZPP.
After you assign an already-created class, the system automatically assigns the charac-
teristics associated with that class. There’s no need to maintain any specific value for
the characteristics (in the Value column) because you’ll do that during the transac-
tional data entry.

10 Batch Management
520
Figure 10.68 Classification View in Material Master
Note
The classification settings you’ve made for the sender material also apply to the recei-
ver material, especially the common characteristics whose values the system should
transfer during batch derivation. Although the preceding example shows classification
details of sender material 2265, we’ve made similar classification settings for receiver
material 2264.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the steps involved in setting up batch derivation,
including maintaining details of sender and receiver materials’ batch details and how a
sender material’s batch data flows into a receiver material’s batch.
Classification: Class with Characteristics
It’s important to understand how a class and characteristics are linked together. To
view the structure of class ZPP and the characteristics associated with it, follow menu
path Cross-Application Components  Classification  Master Data  Classes or use Trans-
action CL02. On the initial screen, enter Class “ZPP” and Class Type “023” (Batch) and
then choose Change.
© 

521
10.8 Batch Derivation
Figure 10.69 shows three characteristics—MM02, MM10, and MM11—whose descrip-
tions appeared or were visible in Figure 10.68. We refer to several instances when the
system interchangeably displays the characteristics or their descriptions. The data type
of characteristic MM10 is CHAR and consists of 1 character, the data type of MM11 is
DATE and has 10 characters, and the data type of MM02 is NUM and has 4 characters.
MM02 also has a unit of measure of mm (millimeters) and two decimal places.
For this example of batch derivation, we’ll use all three characteristics to transfer their
values from a sender material’s batch to a receiver material’s batch.
Figure 10.69 Characteristics Assignment in Class
Batch Derivation: Sender Material
For batch derivation settings for the sender material, including the characteristics, you
follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch
Derivation  Sender Condition Records  Create or use Transaction DVS1. On the initial
screen, in which the standard Strategy Type option is available, the available key com-
binations only offer the options to choose the Sender Material Number radio button or
the Receiving material + Sending material radio button. This key combination enables
you to maintain not just the sender material’s information but also the receiver’s
material. Place the cursor on the Strategy Type field, press (F4) to see the dropdown list,
select condition type BDS1, and press (Enter). Then, the system will open a popup
screen to provide radio buttons to let you select a combination of both receiving and
sending material information on the same screen, plus only sender material number
information. For this example, select the first radio button (Receiving Material + Send-
ing Material), and choose Continue.
On the screen shown in Figure 10.70, enter “2264” in the Receiver Material field and
validity dates in the Valid From and Valid To fields. If validity dates aren’t entered, then
the system automatically sets the validity as from the date of creation of the condition

10 Batch Management
522
record until December 31, 9999. If there’s a business need to use a different validity
than what the system proposes, you should use the Validity option (not shown).
Then, enter Sender Material “2265” in the lower half of the screen. In this section of the
screen, you can enter multiple sender materials for one receiver material. Select Sender
Material 2265 and then choose Details from the top menu bar.
Figure 10.70 Defining Sender-Receiver Relationship
Figure 10.71 shows details about both Sender Material 2265 and Receiver Material 2264.
Here, enter characteristics “MM02,” “MM10,” and “MM11” in the lower half of the
screen. That tells the system to send the characteristic values of these three character-
istics from material 2265 (the sender material) to material 2264 (the receiver material).
These are the same characteristics that you previously maintained in the Classification
view of material masters 2264 and 2265 (refer to Figure 10.68).
Figure 10.71 Attributes of Sender Material
© 

523
10.8 Batch Derivation
The next step is to define the event or instance when the system performs batch deri-
vation for the receiver material. Automatic batch derivation is possible at the time
when a production or process order is released, at goods receipt of a production or pro-
cess order, at goods receipt of a PO, during a usage decision from a quality inspection
process, or at transfer posting between two batches of different materials with com-
mon characteristics. You can also perform manual batch derivation if there is any such
business need. Choose Condition Usage or press (F7).
Select Event 200 (see Figure 10.72) by clicking it once to highlight it; this will enable the
system to perform batch derivation on the release of a production or a process order.
Then, choose Continue and save the settings.
Tips and Tricks
Referring to Figure 10.72, if you are not able to see the event (such as a usage decision,
purchase order, or process order) for which you’d like the system to perform batch der-
ivation, then you can navigate to the configuration menu path to add and activate the
required events. The menu path is SAP IMG  Logistics - General  Batch Management 
Derivation of Batch Data  Define Derivation Events.
Figure 10.72 Choosing Event for Batch Derivation
So far, we’ve covered batch derivation for the sender material while also associating it
with the receiver material. However, the setting for the receiver material is signifi-
cantly more detailed than the setting for the sender material, especially in terms of
how the system responds if batch derivation can’t be performed on the receiver mate-
rial or if one of the characteristic values derived from batch derivation is missing.

10 Batch Management
524
Batch Derivation: Receiver Material
For batch derivation settings for receiver material, including the characteristics, you
follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch
Derivation  Recipient Condition Records  Create or use Transaction DVR1. On the initial
screen, the Strategy Type option is available for the receiver material key combination.
This key combination enables a user to enter not just the receiver material’s informa-
tion but also that of the receiving material type. Place the cursor on the Strategy Type
field and press (F4); the system then brings up Condition Type BDR1 and BDR2 to select
from. Select Condition Type BDR1, press (Enter), and choose the Material Number radio
button in the popup that appears.
Next, enter Receiver Material “2264.” If validity dates aren’t maintained, then the sys-
tem automatically sets the validity from the date of creation of the condition record
until December 31, 9999 (infinity). Then, choose Receiver Material—2264 and choose
Details so that the screen shown in Figure 10.73 appears. Here, enter the same three
characteristics or attributes of class ZPP: “MM02,” “MM10,” and “MM11.” Next, select all
of these characteristics one by one and choose the Rules button located at the bottom
of the screen.
Figure 10.73 Attributes for Characteristics of Receiver Material
© 

525
10.8 Batch Derivation
On the screen shown in Figure 10.74, define the rules for every individual characteristic
that the system needs to follow if it can’t perform batch derivation. In this example, we
set the rules for the first characteristic, MM02, in which we set the control level to
W Warning in case the system finds the Sender field empty. An empty Sender field
means that there is no characteristic value in the sender material’s classification, so
nothing transfers to the receiver material during batch derivation. These warning mes-
sages can also be turned into error messages if you want to ensure handling the batch
derivation–related issues as soon as an error message appears. The second control set-
ting for the same characteristic (MM02) stipulates how the system reacts if it’s able to
find an already maintained value in the characteristic. If the system is to overwrite the
value of this characteristic, it will do so with a warning message or bring up an error
message that doesn’t allow overwriting.
Figure 10.74 Rules for Characteristics of Receiver Material
Repeat the same rules setting for the remaining two characteristics, MM10 and MM11.

sections.title: Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
sections.text:
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
Batch traceability is a key functionality that’s used in almost all industries, including in
discrete manufacturing. SAP delivers the batch where-used list (Transaction MB56) in
the SAP S/4HANA system. This where-used list enables business users to trace the
batch from the finished product to the raw material (from the top down) or from the
raw material to the finished product (from the bottom up). The WIP batch is used to
keep the batch traceability for a process order when several batches of raw material are
consumed and several batches of a finished product are generated.
Transaction MB56 works well in most cases. However, there is a limitation on a process
order with N:N batches. In a standard process order, there is no link between the raw
material batch consumed and the finished product’s batch received in the warehouse;
therefore, the batch where-used list determines all the batches of raw material for each
batch of the finished product. Imagine, for example, that a problem was detected in a
raw material batch after production. In this case, it isn’t possible to identify exactly
which batch of finished product was produced with this problematic batch of raw
material. In previous releases, the only solution for this scenario was to create separate
process orders for each raw material batch, but that required more data entry, ineffi-
cient business processes, and a significant amount of manual work.
In this example, we focus on the WIP batch functionality that is available in the system
for production and process orders in SAP S/4HANA. More specifically, we explain how
to enhance the batch where-used list to show a link between each raw material batch
consumed and the respective finished product batch produced in a process order. Our
example involves a simple process order with two operations and a few components.
In the following sections, we start with the necessary configuration needed to set up
WIP batches, followed by the required master data setup. Then, we transition to the
business processes involved in WIP batches, and we conclude by using the Batch Infor-
mation Cockpit reporting tool to trace WIP batches.

sections.title: Configuration Settings
sections.text:
10.9.1 Configuration Settings
The following are the configuration steps you need to perform before creating the con-
firmation profile.
Activating Work-in-Process Batch Business Functions
Two business functions are related to the WIP batch functionality that you must acti-
vate by using Transaction SFW5: LOG_PP_WIP_BATCH and LOG_PP_WIP_BATCH_02. For the
basic use of the WIP batch and for the scenario covered here, you only need to activate
the first business function. However, you should activate the second business function
as well because it brings additional functionalities for the WIP batch, such as inventory
valuation, use with documentary batches, and use on an order split.

10 Batch Management
530
To activate the business function using the Switch Framework (Transaction SFW5),
select the business function and choose the Activate Changes button.
Warning
You can’t deactivate these business functions after they’re activated.
Activating the Work-in-Process Batch
After you activate the business function, you also need to activate the WIP batch func-
tionality in configuration. To complete this step, use Transaction OMCWB, choose the
Active radio button, and then save.
Warning
Once you activate this configuration setting, it can’t be reversed. In other words, when
a user allows the use of the WIP batch functionality in the SAP system, it isn’t possible
to get back to the original state in which WIP batches can’t be used.
Activating the Original Batch
The next configuration step is to activate original batch reference material. To com-
plete this step, you execute Transaction OMT0. On the initial screen that appears (see
Figure 10.81), check the Original Batch Reference Material box and then save. Doing this
enables the system to use this functionality in master data.
Figure 10.81 Activating Original Batch Reference Material
© 

531
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
Confirmation
Now that the WIP batch and original batch management functionalities are activated,
the next configuration step is to create a new confirmation profile. Because the WIP
batch information must be posted during the process order confirmation, the confir-
mation profile will include the WIP batch–specific subscreens.
To make the necessary configuration settings for the confirmation profile, you use
Transaction OPK1 and choose standard confirmation profile SAP101. On the next screen
(see Figure 10.82), in the Area Selection tab, you need to add areas 8285 WIP Batch:
Goods Receipts, 8290 WIP Batch: Goods Issues, and 8295 WIP Batch: WIP Batch Entry to
the standard Profile SAP101.
Use Transaction OPK0 if the WIP batch process is being implemented in discrete man-
ufacturing (in a production order).
Figure 10.82 Confirmation Profile
Finally, use Transaction OMBB to switch on the option for a synchronous batch where-
used list. On the screen that appears, check the BaWU synchron.posting box for the rel-
evant plant, which in this example is plant 1710.

10 Batch Management
532

sections.title: Master Data Setup
sections.text:
10.9.2 Master Data Setup
Let’s now delve into the requisite master data that you need to set up for WIP batches.
Confirmation Profile in the User Profile
To set the confirmation profile created in the previous step as the default while posting
a confirmation using Transaction COR6N, you check the Standard Prof. (standard pro-
file) box on the initial screen of Transaction OPK1. However, because WIP batching is a
very specific functionality that may not be used in all scenarios, here’s an alternative.
For each business user who posts a confirmation using WIP batch, you can add this con-
firmation profile to the user parameters. User parameters are used to store user-specific
settings. To add a new user parameter, use Transaction SU3, enter “CORUPROF” as the
SET/GET Parameter ID, and enter the previously created confirmation profile (“SAP101”)
as the Parameter value (see Figure 10.83).
Figure 10.83 Confirmation Profile in User Profile
Original Batch in the Material Master
As previously explained, checking the Original Batch Reference Material box in config-
uration activates the OB Management (original batch management) field in the mate-
rial master’s Work scheduling tab (see Figure 10.84).
You must set the value of the OB Management field to 1 (Allowed) for the finished prod-
uct, but you don’t need to set any value for the raw material. Also, note that the Batch
mgmt box has been checked.
© 

533
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
Figure 10.84 Allowing Original Batch Management in the Material Master
Let’s now move to the business processes of WIP batches.

sections.title: Business Processes
sections.text:
10.9.3 Business Processes
The business processes in WIP batches entail creating a process order and then perform-
ing confirmation, in which the WIP batches are created. The system automatically posts
the goods issue and goods receipt. We’ll cover these processes in the following sections.
Process Order
Consider the example shown in Figure 10.85 of a regular process order that appears
after executing Transaction COR2. This screen shows the Material List, in which one
component, 2265, is assigned batch number 0000000130.
Figure 10.85 Process Order with Material List

10 Batch Management
534
To post the confirmation using WIP batches, you need to create a link between the raw
material and the finished product. You don’t need to make any changes to the process
order header or components, and you can directly proceed to order confirmation.
Confirmation and Goods Issuance
When you’re posting a confirmation using the WIP batch confirmation profile, you’ll
notice some of the following details:
 The button to display the Goods Movements overview isn’t displayed.
 The goods receipt goods movement is always proposed, even if automatic goods
receipt isn’t set for the process order.
 The goods issue goods movements are always proposed, even if the Backflush box
isn’t checked for the components.
 All the goods movements are proposed without quantity.
Use Transaction COR6N to access the screen shown in Figure 10.86 and then enter pro-
cess Order “1002208” and its first Phase, which in this example is “0020.” Scroll down
the screen to evaluate the information provided here.
Figure 10.86 Confirmation with WIP Batches
The same three areas—WIP Batch: GI, WIP Batch: GR, and WIP Batch Entry—that you set
in the confirmation profile are displayed here. In the WIP Batch: GI area, the system
may propose several components, of which we choose raw Material 2265 and its
© 

535
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
associated batch 130 for consumption (using Mvmnt Type 261). Select this line item and
then choose the WIP Batch button.
On the popup screen that appears (see Figure 10.87), enter the name of the WIP batch in
the WIP Batch field (e.g., “WIP_B_006”). In this process, you establish a link between the
raw material batch consumed and the WIP batch. Then, choose Continue.
Figure 10.87 WIP Batch
The screen shown in Figure 10.88 prompts you to maintain the characteristic values of
WIP batch WIP_B_006. Figure 10.89 shows the newly created WIP Batch WIP_B_006.
Figure 10.88 Maintaining Characteristic Values of the WIP Batch

10 Batch Management
536
Figure 10.89 Yield Entry for the WIP Batch
Next, you maintain the standard confirmation details, such as the yield, scrap, rework,
and associated activities (e.g., machine hours, labor hours), as you normally do in the
confirmation process.
Work in Process Batch Goods Receipt
As explained previously in this section, the goods receipt movement is proposed by
default in the WIP Batch: GR section; however, it’s proposed without a quantity, so
you’ll need to input the quantity. After that, select the goods receipt item and the
respective WIP Batch, as shown in Figure 10.90, where you first select WIP_B_006 and
then choose the WIPB->GR button. (If the batch for the finished good was already cre-
ated at the time of creating or releasing the process order, then you don’t need to per-
form this step.) This will bring up the screen shown in Figure 10.91.
In Figure 10.91, notice that the system has created GR Batch number 0000000136 for
finished good Material 2264 in the WIP Batch: GR section. You can now save this confir-
mation, and because the WIP batch was already linked to the raw material, a link
between the raw material and the finished product material is created.
© 

537
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
Figure 10.90 Assigning the WIP Batch to the Finished Good Batch
Figure 10.91 WIP Batch Goods Receipt
This is how the system links a raw material’s batch to a WIP batch and then to a finished
good’s batch for process order 1002208: Raw material 2265 (batch number 130): WIP
batch (WIP_B_006): Finished good 2264 (batch number 136). We’ll see the same linkup
when we cover reporting in Section 10.9.4.

When you’re posting goods receipt of a finished goods batch, a few more screens or
popups may appear where you can maintain additional details, such as the date of

Note
Now that you have a basic understanding of WIP batches and how they work, we sug-
gest exploring several additional features and functionalities that are available to han-
dle all business processes.
For example, in Figure 10.90, we showed you how to link the WIP batch with a goods
receipt, but what if a user mistakenly creates a wrong link? To undo or delink this link-

Access the Batch Information Cockpit screen (see Figure 10.92) by using Transaction

Figure 10.93 shows Batch 0000000136 for Material 2264, which meets the selection cri-
teria. Choose Batch 0000000136 on the left-hand side of the screen, then select Usage
from the Details dropdown icon located in the top-right corner of the screen.
© 

539
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
For batch 0000000136 for the finished good 2264, Figure 10.93 shows the Top-Down
Analysis of process order 1002208. However, these screen details still don’t show the

The next section looks at the Batch Information Cockpit and how you can use it to ana-

sections.title: Batch Information Cockpit
sections.text:
10.10 Batch Information Cockpit
In process industries, such as food and pharmaceuticals, traceability of the ingredients
or components within a production or procurement process is of paramount impor-
tance. Batch traceability across logistics and supply chain processes is also a legal
requirement in many industries and a good manufacturing practice to maintain in
case there is a batch recall. Companies often develop complex custom reports to view
and analyze batch traceability when the Batch Information Cockpit can serve the same
purpose.
With the Batch Information Cockpit, you can do the following:
 You can perform top-down and bottom-up traceability analysis. Top-down traceabil-
ity analysis of a finished good traces all the raw, packaging, and semifinished mate-
rials that were used in making the finished product’s batch. With bottom-up trace-
ability analysis, you trace the ingredients or raw materials and trace all the finished
goods that were made using these ingredients or raw materials batches.
 You can access the batch master directly from the Batch Information Cockpit to dis-
play and change information related to the batch while performing the traceability
analysis of the batch.
 You can display the stock information for the batch.
 You can display the stock position of a material and its associated batches.
To access the Batch Information Cockpit, follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions 
Batch Management  Batch Information Cockpit or use Transaction BMBC. Figure 10.94
shows the initial screen where you set parameters that the system then considers to
bring up relevant batches of materials. Notice the various tabs available on the right-
hand side, which you can use to enter specific information. The left-hand side of the
screen provides search functionality.
For this example, enter Material “2264” and Plant “1710.” Then, choose Selection and
then Execute Selection. This brings up the screen shown in Figure 10.95, where the left-
hand side shows that the system is able to find the relevant material-plant-batch com-
bination. The right-hand side appears when a user selects Usage from the Details drop-
down list.
Figure 10.95 also shows the bottom-up analysis of batch 130 of material 2264 in plant
1710. It shows that this batch was consumed in process order 1002208, which in turn
produced batch 136. The raw materials procured externally from suppliers will show up
with the material document numbers in the same top-down analysis.
Note
We suggest that you explore several features and functionalities of the Batch Informa-
tion Cockpit to take advantage of the large amount of information it stores.
© 

541
10.10 Batch Information Cockpit
For example, in the Usage tab of Figure 10.95, you can choose whether the system
should bring up a top-down list or bottom-up list. Similarly, you can gain comprehen-
sive visibility into all batches that have expired or whose expiration dates are
approaching.
Figure 10.94 Batch Information Cockpit
Figure 10.95 Bottom-Up Analysis

10 Batch Management
542
In Figure 10.96, the Batch Information Cockpit has been run, and several batches have
been selected. The materials are shown, and you can display the matches by highlight-
ing the material. The system will then display information regarding the batch on a
main screen. The tabs represent the number of different screens with information on
this batch.
Figure 10.96 Central Area of the Batch Information Cockpit

sections.title: Electronic Batch Record
sections.text:
10.11 Electronic Batch Record
In highly regulated industries, such as pharmaceuticals, there’s often a legal require-
ment to maintain a complete trail and record of all the batches used in the logistics and
supply chain of manufacturing a product. Often, it’s not just important to keep records
on the batches received from the procurement process or batches consumed in the
production process; you must maintain a complete record of various quality inspec-
tions that took place in the manufacturing process. The electronic batch record, or batch
record for short, captures these inspection details.
You access the screen shown in Figure 10.97 by following menu path Logistics  Central
Functions  Batch Management  Tools  Batch Record or using Transaction COEBR. On
the left-hand side, you can see a complete trail for material 2264 and its associated
Batch 136. As you drill down in the same node, the related process orders will appear: (1)
the two types of quality inspections (04 for finished goods inspection and 03 for in-pro-
cess inspection) if the PP-QM integration is in place and (2) the Material List (BOM) used
in the production process.
In the upper-right corner of the same screen, you can only approve an electronic batch
record for archiving with the Approve button if the statuses of various elements (e.g.,
process or process order, inspection lots) are set as required for electronic archiving.
Here, the system also guides you to check all the prerequisites for electronic batch
records. After approval, you can archive an electronic batch record by clicking the
Archive button.
© 

543
10.11 Electronic Batch Record
Figure 10.97 Electronic Batch Record
The lower-right corner shows comprehensive details of the electronic batch record in
PDF format.
Note
We recommend that you engage an SAP NetWeaver consultant to ensure that the nec-
essary authorizations required for archiving electronic batches are granted. The SAP
NetWeaver consultant will also set up a document repository required to store the
electronic batch records being archived.
In some industries, such as consumer products and automotive suppliers, there are
legal requirements to store where-used data of materials used for production and
delivered to customers. Enabling recall actions is a mandatory and critical issue for
these industries. Data can be recorded by managing all relevant materials in batches,
but this method has a negative impact on data volume. When turning on batch man-
agement, entering a batch number becomes obligatory for all goods movements. Then
all inventory postings must be executed on the batch level, and labor costs for inven-
tory management will increase.
With the documentary batch record, it’s important to ensure that a partial stock of a
material is traceable without having to maintain a batch’s data. This reduces the effort
and complexities involved in maintaining batch data. The documentary batch record
finds greater use in automotive and consumer goods industries but is unsuitable for
use in dangerous goods.
You can perform documentary batch processing in the following ways:
 You can enter documentary batches during goods movements.
 In a process order, you can enter documentary batches only at backflush.
 In embedded EWM, you must enable entering documentary batches during trans-
port order confirmation.

10 Batch Management
544

sections.title: Summary
sections.text:
10.12 Summary
Batch management is important to a large number of process-based industries. It has
evolved from just the identification of groups of items into a process that allows com-
panies to perform product recalls, select and sell by batch characteristics, and identify
expiring stock. As the drive for competitive advantage continues, companies will fur-
ther investigate how batch information can lower production time and hasten delivery
materials to customers.
Chapter 11 will discuss sales and operations planning.
©
